findings.summary: OCR worker class is DocumentProcessor but classifier worker class is ClassificationProcessor — inconsistent naming pattern
evidence: ocr/worker.py defines DocumentProcessor while classifier/worker.py defines ClassificationProcessor — the prefix naming convention differs (Document vs Classification)
findings.suggestion: Rename to OcrProcessor / ClassificationProcessor or DocumentProcessor / DocumentClassifier for consistent naming across sibling modules

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider uses _reset_stats() method while OpenAIProvider initializes stats dict in __init__ — sibling providers follow different patterns
evidence: ClassificationProvider._reset_stats() reinitializes self._stats dict on each classify_text call; OpenAIProvider initializes stats once in __init__ and accumulates across calls
findings.suggestion: Align stats lifecycle: either both reset per-call or both accumulate, making the behavioral contract consistent across providers

findings.summary: OCR daemon uses module-level _process_document function; classifier daemon uses inline closure in main()
evidence: ocr/daemon.py: 'def _process_document(doc: dict, settings: Settings) -> None:' at module level; classifier/daemon.py: 'def process_document(doc: dict) -> None:' as closure inside main()
findings.suggestion: Use the same pattern in both daemons: either both module-level functions or both closures

findings.summary: Classifier has a dedicated constants.py but OCR scatters its constants (OCR_ERROR_MARKER, TRANSCRIPTION_PROMPT) across text_assembly.py and prompts.py
evidence: classifier package has constants.py with GENERIC_DOCUMENT_TYPES, BLACKLISTED_TAGS, ERROR_PHRASES; OCR package has OCR_ERROR_MARKER in text_assembly.py and TRANSCRIPTION_PROMPT in prompts.py with no constants.py
findings.suggestion: Either move OCR constants to an ocr/constants.py for consistency, or acknowledge the difference is intentional given the smaller constant surface in OCR

findings.summary: Classifier daemon docstring references wrong module path for iter helper
evidence: _iter_docs_to_classify docstring says 'Delegates to the shared :func:`~common.tags.iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag`' but the actual import is from common.document_iter

findings.summary: Both src/ocr/prompts.py and src/classifier/prompts.py lack test files
evidence: No test_prompts.py exists in tests/unit/ocr/ or tests/unit/classifier/; prompts.py files contain schema definitions (CLASSIFICATION_JSON_SCHEMA) and structured constants that could drift from actual API expectations
findings.suggestion: Add at minimum a schema validation test for CLASSIFICATION_JSON_SCHEMA ensuring required fields match ClassificationResult fields

findings.summary: Worker tests patch internal functions (truncate_content_by_pages, enrich_tags, normalize_language) making them brittle to refactoring
evidence: test_worker.py patches classifier.worker.truncate_content_by_pages, classifier.worker.enrich_tags, classifier.worker.normalize_language, classifier.worker.update_custom_fields, classifier.worker.needs_error_tag — these are implementation details that could change without the behavior changing
findings.suggestion: Reduce internal patching by testing through the public process() interface with realistic inputs; use e2e-style fixtures (as test_classifier_workflow.py already does) for more of the unit test scenarios

findings.summary: retry decorator is tested but _sleep_backoff calls time.sleep which is not mocked in some tests, creating fragility
evidence: retry.py calls time.sleep inside _sleep_backoff; tests that exercise retry paths need to mock time.sleep or risk slow test execution
findings.suggestion: Make the sleep function injectable (as daemon_loop already does) or ensure all retry tests consistently mock time.sleep

findings.summary: E2e tests mock PaperlessClient and ClassificationProvider so they test the same boundaries as integration tests
evidence: test_classifier_workflow.py creates _make_stateful_client (MagicMock) and _make_mock_classifier (MagicMock) — these are the same mocked boundaries as the integration tests, making the e2e label misleading
findings.suggestion: Either rename to integration tests, or add true e2e tests that use httpx mocking (respx) to test the actual HTTP request/response flow

findings.summary: conftest.py provides both settings and settings_obj fixtures; many tests import make_settings_obj directly instead of using fixtures
evidence: conftest.py defines settings and settings_obj fixtures; test_worker.py files import make_settings_obj directly from factories and build their own settings, bypassing the shared fixtures entirely
findings.suggestion: Either remove unused conftest fixtures or refactor test helpers to use them, reducing the number of paths to create test settings

findings.summary: common/document_iter.py is essentially a tag-based document filter but lives as a separate module from common/tags.py
evidence: document_iter.py imports extract_tags and remove_stale_queue_tag from tags.py; its only function iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag is purely tag-based filtering logic that is closely related to the tag lifecycle in tags.py
findings.suggestion: Consider merging document_iter.py into tags.py or renaming document_iter.py to something that better signals its tag-filtering role (e.g. tag_iteration.py)

findings.summary: common/ has 15 modules mixing infrastructure (retry, shutdown, concurrency), API clients (paperless, llm), and domain helpers (tags, claims, utils)
evidence: common/ contains 15 .py files: bootstrap, claims, concurrency, config, daemon_loop, document_iter, library_setup, llm, logging_config, paperless, preflight, retry, shutdown, stale_lock, tags, utils — all at the same level
findings.suggestion: This is borderline given the project size (~37 source files), but could benefit from subpackages like common/infra/ (retry, shutdown, concurrency, logging_config) and common/pipeline/ (tags, claims, document_iter, stale_lock) if the package grows further

findings.summary: In truncate_content_by_pages, first segment always starts at index 0 even if the first page header is not at the beginning, potentially double-counting preamble text
evidence: Lines 173-175: 'start = 0 if idx == 0 else match.start()' — when idx==0 the segment starts at 0 (before the first page header), but when the same page is later selected via include_indices, the preamble text before the first header is always included regardless of whether page 1 is in the include set
findings.suggestion: Document this behavior explicitly or separate the preamble from page 1 content so truncation decisions are clearer

findings.summary: get_or_create_tag_ids acquires and releases the lock per tag instead of holding it for the entire batch, risking inconsistent state between iterations
evidence: Lines 258-281: 'for tag in dedupe_tags(tags): with self._lock:' — the lock is acquired/released for each tag in the loop, so another thread could interleave tag creation and cause duplicate creates
findings.suggestion: Hold the lock for the entire tag resolution loop, or document why per-tag locking is acceptable (e.g., duplicate creates are handled by the catch-refresh-retry pattern)

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._create_with_compat creates shallow copies of params dict on each compat retry but reuses the same messages list
evidence: Lines 143-144: 'params = dict(params)' creates a shallow copy, but the 'messages' list inside is shared across all retry iterations — this works only because messages are not mutated, but the intent is fragile
findings.suggestion: Add a comment clarifying that the shallow copy is sufficient because only top-level keys are removed, or use copy.deepcopy for safety

findings.summary: OCR worker uses f-string in log message argument instead of structured logging field
evidence: Line 233: 'log.warning(f"OCR produced {reason}; marking error", doc_id=self.doc_id)' — uses f-string interpolation in the event message instead of passing 'reason' as a structured field
findings.suggestion: Change to log.warning('OCR produced error content; marking error', doc_id=self.doc_id, reason=reason) for consistent structured logging

findings.summary: Settings class has type annotations at class level that are never used as actual class attributes — they serve only as documentation
evidence: Lines 28-81: class-level annotations like 'PAPERLESS_URL: str' and 'PAPERLESS_TOKEN: str' are not used by Python at runtime; all actual values are set in __init__ via self.ATTRIBUTE assignments
findings.suggestion: Either use a dataclass/attrs pattern that enforces the annotations, or remove the class-level annotations to avoid confusion about whether they serve a runtime purpose
